perturbation.original_text: 10. Term. This Agreement shall commence on the firs t date that both parties have signed the Agreement (the "Effective Date") and sha ll terminate 3 years following the Effective Date. Either party may terminate this Agr eement at any time, given that the party provides the other party with at least 90 day s prior written notice. Following termination of this agreement, confidentiality shal l still be maintained for two years following the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Term. This Agreement shall commence on the firs t date that both parties have signed the Agreement (the "Effective Date") and sha ll terminate 3 years following the Effective Date. Either party may terminate this Agr eement at any time, given that the party provides the other party with at least 10 day s prior written notice. Following termination of this agreement, confidentiality shal l still be maintained for two years following the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires a 90-day notice for termination. The modified text shortens it to 10 days, which could be unreasonably short depending on the circumstances and potentially violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing - New York Law
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract.  A 10-day notice period for termination might be deemed unreasonably short, potentially violating this covenant if it deprives the other party of a reasonable opportunity to wind down affairs or find a replacement, considering the nature of the confidential information and the business relationship.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 1-304 Obligation of Good Faith Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 1, PART 3 Section 1--304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the termination notice period from 90 days to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this could violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under New York law if the shorter notice is deemed unreasonable. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a 10-day notice might be unreasonably short and violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if it deprives the other party of a reasonable opportunity to wind down affairs. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing UCC § 1-304, states that "Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Thus, a 10-day notice period that doesn't allow for good faith performance could create a legal contradiction because it is inconsistent with the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Return of Information. Upon termination or expir ation of the Agreement, or upon written request by either party, each party shall p romptly return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof, or destroy all Confidential Information and copies thereof and certify its destruction to the Provider .
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Return of Information. Upon termination or expir ation of the Agreement, or upon written request by either party, each party shall p romptly return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof, or destroy all Confidential Information and copies thereof and certify its destruction to the Provider within 90 days.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that upon termination, information should be returned promptly. The change adds a specific 90-day timeline.  Depending on the nature of the information and state law, this may not be prompt enough. If the information is related to trade secrets and NY law is used, it should be returned immediately.
perturbation.law_citation: Integrated Cash Mgmt. Servs., Inc. v. Digital Transactions, Inc., 920 F.2d 171, 173 (2d Cir. 1990)
perturbation.law_explanation: While there is no explicit 'return time' stated in New York's trade secret law, delaying the return of a trade secret, as allowed by the modified text, conflicts with the implied duty to protect the trade secret owner's interests. Trade secrets should be returned or destroyed immediately to prevent misappropriation, so a 90-day delay is a violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 56. Summary Judgment | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 56. Summary Judgment (a) Motion for Summary Judgment or Partial Summary Judgment. A party may move for summary judgment, identifying each claim or defense — or the part of each claim or defense — on which summary judgment is sought. The court shall grant summary judgment if the movant shows that there is no genuine dispute as to any material fact and the movant is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. The court should state on the record the reasons for granting or denying the motion. (b) Time to File a Motion. Unless a different time is set by local rule or the court orders otherwise, a party may file a motion for summary judgment at any time until 30 days after the close of all discovery. (c) Procedures. (1) Supporting Factual Positions. A party asserting that a fact cannot be or is genuinely disputed must support the assertion by: (A) citing to particular parts of materials in the record, including depositions, documents, electronically stored information, affidavits or declarations, stipulations (including those made for purposes of the motion only), admissions, interrogatory answers, or other materials; or (B) showing that the materials cited do not establish the absence or presence of a genuine dispute, or that an adverse party cannot produce admissible evidence to support the fact. (2) Objection That a Fact Is Not Supported by Admissible Evidence. A party may object that the material cited to support or dispute a fact cannot be presented in a form that would be admissible in evidence. (3) Materials Not Cited. The court need consider only the cited materials, but it may consider other materials in the record. (4) Affidavits or Declarations. An affidavit or declaration used to support or oppose a motion must be made on personal knowledge, set out facts that would be admissible in evidence, and show that the affiant or declarant is competent to testify on the matters stated. (d) When Facts Are Unavailable to the Nonmovant. If a nonmovant shows by affidavit or declaration that, for specified reasons, it cannot present facts essential to justify its opposition, the court may: (1) defer considering the motion or deny it; (2) allow time to obtain affidavits or declarations or to take discovery; or (3) issue any other appropriate order. (e) Failing to Properly Support or Address a Fact. If a party fails to properly support an assertion of fact or fails to properly address another party’s assertion of fact as required by Rule 56(c) , the court may: (1) give an opportunity to properly support or address the fact; (2) consider the fact undisputed for purposes of the motion; (3) grant summary judgment if the motion and supporting materials — including the facts considered undisputed — show that the movant is entitled to it; or (4) issue any other appropriate order. (f) Judgment Independent of the Motion. After giving notice and a reasonable time to respond, the court may: (1) grant summary judgment for a nonmovant; (2) grant the motion on grounds not raised by a party;or (3) consider summary judgment on its own after identifying for the parties material facts that may not be genuinely in dispute. (g) Failing to Grant All the Requested Relief. If the court does not grant all the relief requested by the motion, it may enter an order stating any material fact
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day delay for returning or destroying confidential information. The law explanation indicates that this delay conflicts with the implied duty to protect the trade secret owner's interests and should be done immediately to prevent misappropriation. This falls under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where a time-sensitive obligation is set that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: 1. Confidential Information. As used herein, "Confi dential Information" shall mean any and all oral and written proprietary informatio n provided by one party (the "Provider") to the other (the "Recipient"), includi ng but not limited to financial documents, customer lists, development plans, busin ess plans, trade secrets, and intellectual property. "Confidential Information" d oes not include information that (i) is generally available to the public on the date of th is agreement, (ii) becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure not otherwise permissible hereunder, (iii) was in the Recipient's possession prior to disclosure by the Provider, except to the extent that the Recipie nt received th e information in violation of any requirement of confidentiality of which the Recipie nt was aware or reasonably should have been aware, or (iv) is made available to the R ecipient lawfully and in good faith by a third party who Recipient reasonably believes is not under an obligation to keep such information confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Confidential Information. As used herein, "Confi dential Information" shall mean only written proprietary information n provided by one party (the "Provider") to the other (the "Recipient"), includi ng but not limited to financial documents, customer lists, development plans, busin ess plans, trade secrets, and intellectual property. "Confidential Information" d oes not include information that (i) is generally available to the public on the date of th is agreement, (ii) becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure not otherwise permissible hereunder, (iii) was in the Recipient's possession prior to disclosure by the Provider, except to the extent that the Recipie nt received th e information in violation of any requirement of confidentiality of which the Recipie nt was aware or reasonably should have been aware, or (iv) is made available to the R ecipient lawfully and in good faith by a third party who Recipient reasonably believes is not under an obligation to keep such information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of Confidential Information includes both 'oral and written' information. The change removes 'oral'. In cases of trade secrets, oral disclosures can be protected; by limiting the definition to only written, the contract could unintentionally waive protection for orally communicated trade secrets.
perturbation.law_citation: Ashland Mgmt. Inc. v. Janien, 82 N.Y.2d 395, 407, 626 N.E.2d 296, 302 (1993)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law recognizes that trade secrets can be communicated orally. By defining "Confidential Information" to include only *written* information, the modified text potentially contradicts New York trade secret law. The changed text makes it harder for the provider to protect their trade secret because they have to have it in writing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ashland Mgt. v. Janien :: 1993 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Ashland Mgt. v. Janien Annotate this Case 82 N.Y.2d 395 (1993) 624 N.E.2d 1007 604 N.Y.S.2d 912 Ashland Management Incorporated, Appellant, v. C. Christopher Janien, Respondent. (And a Third-Party Action.) Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued October 14, 1993. Decided November 22, 1993. Paul, Hastings, Janofsky & Walker, New York City (Lawrence I. Weinstein, Lisa M. Buckley and Roger M. Milgrim of counsel), for appellant. Davis & Gilbert, New York City (Michael C. Lasky, Bruce M. Ginsberg and Howard Weingrad of counsel), for respondent. Chief Judge KAYE and Judges TITONE, HANCOCK, JR., BELLACOSA, SMITH and LEVINE concur. *399SIMONS, J. This litigation involves a dispute between an investment management firm and a former employee. The employee developed a mathematical model for determining investment strategy and sought unsuccessfully to sell it to the firm. A dispute arose during the negotiations and plaintiff, Ashland Management Inc., instituted this action seeking to foreclose defendant from using his model. It alleged that the model is based upon trade secrets defendant misappropriated from the firm. Defendant claims he and plaintiff had agreed upon a contract for the use of the model and that plaintiff breached the contract. Defendant, therefore, counterclaimed seeking damages. After a nonjury trial, Supreme Court found that the parties had entered into a contract and that plaintiff had breached it. Accordingly, the court awarded defendant damages of $625,000 for lost profits. It held further that the information available to defendant did not constitute a trade secret of Ashland and therefore defendant was not guilty of misappropriation. The Appellate Division, after a minor modification, affirmed the judgment. The principal legal questions presented concern the availability of damages for lost profits under the rule stated in our two decisions in Ken
perturbation.justification: The change from 'oral and written' to 'written' in the definition of Confidential Information contradicts the potential protection of orally communicated trade secrets under New York law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2, *Ashland Mgt. v. Janien*, suggests that trade secrets are a recognized legal concept in New York. The Law Explanation states New York law recognizes that trade secrets can be communicated orally. By limiting the definition to only written information, the modified text makes it harder for the provider to protect their trade secret because they have to have it in writing. Therefore, there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Care. The Recipient will use at least as much ca re in maintaining the confidentiality of any disclosed Information as tha t party uses in maintaining its own Confidential Information, and the Recipient will us e at least reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Care. The Recipient will use ordinary care in maintaining the confidentiality of any disclosed Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a higher standard of care ('at least as much care as that party uses in maintaining its own Confidential Information, and the Recipient will use at least reasonable care'). The modified text lowers the standard to 'ordinary care', which may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law, if the Provider uses significantly more than ordinary care to protect its own information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Trade Secret Law (implied standard of care)
perturbation.law_citation: North Atlantic Instruments, Inc. v. Haber, 188 F.3d 38, 44 (2d Cir. 1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Second Circuit states that New York trade secret law "does not require an employer to establish that it took extreme measures to protect the secrecy of its information”, the requirement of merely 'ordinary care' may fall short of the reasonable efforts required to protect a trade secret, depending on the circumstances, thereby contradicting trade secret protection requirements.
perturbation.justification: The modified text lowers the standard of care from 'at least as much care as that party uses in maintaining its own Confidential Information, and the Recipient will use at least reasonable care' to 'ordinary care.' This change can contradict trade secret protection requirements. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that 'the requirement of merely 'ordinary care' may fall short of the reasonable efforts required to protect a trade secret.' This represents a weakening of the original obligation, potentially failing to protect trade secrets as required under law.
